content.text: Author’s noteNote: This quiz does not comply with best practices for when you should use a non-member or member function. The goal is to test whether you understand how to convert non-member functions to member functions.

Note: This quiz does not comply with best practices for when you should use a non-member or member function. The goal is to test whether you understand how to convert non-member functions to member functions.

Question #3In the prior quiz solution, why was the Fraction constructor madeexplicit?Show SolutionMaking the constructor explicit prevents it from being used to create aFractionvia an implicit conversion with a single value. This helps prevent nonsense likef1.multiply(true).f1.multiply(true)requires thattruebe implicitly converted to aFraction. Normally the compiler would useFraction(int numerator=0, int denominator=1)for this, but if we make that constructor explicit, it is not eligible to be used for implicit conversions. Since the compiler will then be unable to find a way to converttrueinto aFraction, it will emit a compilation error.Question #4In the prior quiz question, why might it be better to leavegetFraction()andprint()as non-members?Show SolutionWith the non-member version ofgetFraction(), we can define and initialize a Fraction in a single step. The member version requires two steps, as we must first create an object and then call the member function on it. It also prints application-specific text prompts for the user to follow.By movingprint()to be a non-member (and using access functions to access the members), we remove the function from the interface of the class, making the core functionality of the class less cluttered. It also means that if the implementation of the class is ever changed, we don’t need to consider whetherprint()needs to be updated, so long as the interface isn’t changed (since we know it can’t access any data members directly).Next lesson15.1The hidden “this” pointer and member function chainingBack to table of contentsPrevious lesson14.17Constexpr aggregates and classes
